8:25 it's called 'ikan terubuk' in Malay or toli shad or Chinese herring (Tenualosa toli), a highly prized fish among Malaysians for its meat and eggs.

It's a really good fish for grilling as the meat is dense and fatty, though you have to be careful eating it bcoz of the long and tiny bones. Goes really well with the air asam and sambal kicap for the grilled fish.

author: @chucktz469
comment: Ok this is my third vlog I've seen reviewing Malaysian cuisines and every video has totally impressed me in such a way I have grown intrest and now so invested into to visiting this country. I mean, the food is my type of genre as far as the herbs, chillies, spices, samba, curries, gravies, bbq, local veggies, & fresh fruits. However, those glazed barbeque wings looks phenomenal. The clay pot rice and chicken roasted over the charcoal pit is so satisfying watching. I know flavors when I see it and Malaysia has it embedded in their culture no doubt about it. The locally caught marinated fresh fish wrapped in the banana leaf prepared over the buttered griddle intensifies my cravings simultaneously. Let's not forget Mark's favored choice of the combination dish Nassi' malak' (forgive me if misspelled) is an variety of Malaysia's most desired ingredients infused with such tasty condiments on one plate. Wow, what a amazing review, Mark shared. I'm definitely considering planning a visit to experience this diverse country of excellence. Thanks Mark for this exclusive content of Malaysian cuisines!! Wishing you safe travels always and many more blessings to come for you & your family!! Anticipating on your next virtual treat like always... Cheers my friend!! 🥥🍛🧉

author: @nakhihit9408
comment:
For the ikan bakar part, I think it is better you go for restaurant that serve the actual ikan bakar. I would recommend anywhere in Kuala Perlis, located in the state of Perlis. It is like 6 hours drive from KL though but I guarantee you it is worth it. They only serve the dishes during night, so you will need to get yourself somewhere to stay for the night.
In respective order, get yourself anything between siakap (asian sea bass), jenahak (snapper), or gerut (saddle grunt) with the gerut being the best among them all. You will want to ask for 'masak santan' which is coconut cream gravy on top of the fish.
I am just grateful to be born a Malaysian, get to taste those coconut cream gravy on a gerut, what a fascinating meal ever created!

author: @chucktz469
comment: Ok this is my third vlog I've seen reviewing Malaysian cuisines and every video has totally impressed me in such a way I have grown intrest and now so invested into to visiting this country. I mean, the food is my type of genre as far as the herbs, chillies, spices, samba, curries, gravies, bbq, local veggies, & fresh fruits. However, those glazed barbeque wings looks phenomenal. The clay pot rice and chicken roasted over the charcoal pit is so satisfying watching. I know flavors when I see it and Malaysia has it embedded in their culture no doubt about it. The locally caught marinated fresh fish wrapped in the banana leaf prepared over the buttered griddle intensifies my cravings simultaneously. Let's not forget Mark's favored choice of the combination dish Nassi' malak' (forgive me if misspelled) is an variety of Malaysia's most desired ingredients infused with such tasty condiments on one plate. Wow, what a amazing review, Mark shared. I'm definitely considering planning a visit to experience this diverse country of excellence. Thanks Mark for this exclusive content of Malaysian cuisines!! Wishing you safe travels always and many more blessings to come for you & your family!! Anticipating on your next virtual treat like always... Cheers my friend!! 🥥🍛🧉

author: @nakhihit9408
comment:
For the ikan bakar part, I think it is better you go for restaurant that serve the actual ikan bakar. I would recommend anywhere in Kuala Perlis, located in the state of Perlis. It is like 6 hours drive from KL though but I guarantee you it is worth it. They only serve the dishes during night, so you will need to get yourself somewhere to stay for the night.
In respective order, get yourself anything between siakap (asian sea bass), jenahak (snapper), or gerut (saddle grunt) with the gerut being the best among them all. You will want to ask for 'masak santan' which is coconut cream gravy on top of the fish.
I am just grateful to be born a Malaysian, get to taste those coconut cream gravy on a gerut, what a fascinating meal ever created!

author: @kvngds
comment:
Sonny, you’ve just scratched the surface of Malaysia’s insane food culture in this video! 😍 But if you really want to level up your Malaysian food adventure — you need to go to SABAH and SARAWAK, Borneo! 🗺🔥

There, you’ll find a treasure trove of flavors that don’t even exist in Semenanjung — wild, earthy, indigenous dishes like hinava (our Bornean ceviche!), pansuh (meat cooked in bamboo), linopot, umai, ambuyat, and so much more! 🌿🔥🐗🍚 Every dish has a story, a tribe, and a soul behind it.

And if you love trying local drinks, just wait till you taste our traditional rice wines and herbal concoctions — made with love, tradition, and sometimes a little kick! 🍶🌾✨

Trust me, East Malaysia is not just another destination — it’s a whole new food universe. Come explore the culture, the people, the flavors — you’ll be blown away. 💥✈🇲🇾 #Sabah #Sarawak #BorneoBites #BestEverFoodReviewShowInBorneo

author: @iamnotmental
comment:
typical room service here, you pay a lot of money for lobster (they give you half a lobster), so you get charged for a full lobster
you pay a lot of money for caviar (they give you fish roe instead, the green eggs could be any fish, not the sturgeon black eggs the genuine caviar they don't give you that) so you get charged for official caviar which is sturgeon price.
they present a lot of cheap random dipping sauces and crackers all presented so nicely to confuse you that you are actually getting ripped off, looks pretty though doesn't it -.-
also the "Moët & Chandon Brut Impérial NV Champagne" whole bottle is worth $41.50 USD, it is the cheap sh*t yet you get charged for the official champagne.
stop listening to whatever you hear, especially from you tube Influencers and do your own research and tell me i'm wrong.

tl:dr it's not the you tubers fault, these hotels know what they are doing, the customer doesn't.

author: @MzClementine
comment:
I find stingray to be very ammonia-like. It must be prepared and cooked properly. How I have no idea. My mom was magic with stingray. Myself. Shrimp, clams oysters lobster fish I can handle but the stingray. I just can't do it. You cook me stingray I'll tell you Oh that's stingray. Because it has that flavoring. That very astringent ammonia-like flavor. Maybe it's with my genes .. Because my stepdad couldn't taste it. My mom could. And it reminds me of certain individuals that will eat cilantro taste soap. But others don't. It has to do with your genetics.
🤔 Something to ponder 🧐

If anyone has an ever made oyster stew.. basically, make a bechamel, milk whipping cream roasted garlic regular garlic. Onion scallions I love cilantro in mine. And whatever seafood you're going to throw in. I love saving my oysters for the very last. Top with fresh grated nutmeg. Cook your onion and garlic until translucent Then add in, your bechamel, your cream your milk your roasted head of garlic. Blend it. Blend it up. Make it silky smooth. Then throw in your favorite fish your favorite clams your favorite shrimp and then your oysters. Muscles whatever you want. It is so delicious. I love finishing it with clotted cream.

My husband, his mother made an oyster stew. Not bad a quick throw together out of canned ingredients. Well I learned his favorite mom's recipe. And then, I kicked it up a notch. You can also use coconut milk or coconut cream. Phenomenal to any cream-based seafood soup. You can also add in ginger. Lemongrass. There are different varieties that you can do with this simple meal base. Especially within a white chowder. I love it so much. Use chicken stock. If you want it thinner. I like mine thick. You can even add little new potatoes. It is a meal that cooks up super quick super easy and is phenomenally flavorful.

The raccoon with the cotton candy. Poor thing.😅😂😅 Oh I felt so bad the first time I saw that. Poor thing. Got to wash my food off where to go?😂

I would never trust a skinny chef. With that said. I'm skinny. But I love to cook. And I can cook. I just can't eat everything everyone else can. I saw the chef and was like Oh he's a good chef. And then my brain was like and you are skinny. Why is my brain doing that. How dare you think of me that way brain.😂😂😂😅😂 Sorry just beating myself up. And I shouldn't.

author: @Angus_MacKinnon
comment: Hello Sonny. This Scotsman served in the British Army in Malaya during the Malay Emergency, which later morphed into the Indonesian Confrontation of Malaysia in the 1960s. I served in Malaya for 2 years and in that time; I became "HOOKED" on Char Kway Teow. I ate it at every opportunity and today I love in Australia where my wife and I visit a Malaysian Restaurant in Melbourne, Victoria Australia to have our weekly "hit" of CHAR KWAY TEOW. We pay AUD$16.50 fore a plate of that delicious meal which translates to 51.49 Malaysian Ringits, and in Malaysia, you paid (I presume USD 1.50) that translates to 7.17 Malaysian Ringits. To be honest with you, Malaysian food is not my favourite HOWEVER, I could eat Char Kway Teow every day and never get sick of it. Cheers mate!

author: @sethukumaran7876
comment: At 43:10, the dish is not a roti, chapati, or paratha; the correct name is 'parotta.' There are various types of parotta, and the one featured in the video is a fried parotta, partially fried in a substantial amount of oil or ghee. It originated in the Tamil-populated Jaffna region of Sri Lanka and was later introduced in Tamil Nadu. Parotta is a renowned culinary delight in southern India. It's important to note that many people often mistake it for 'Paratha,' a famous North Indian dish."

author: @marcuschoy803
comment:
the sour flavour comes from the broth which has tamarind slices boiled with the Mackeral Fish. The torchlight flower(Bunga Kantan) only provides the fragrant of the Laksa. The saltish fishy flavor comes from the Prawn paste.

But this laksa is a Indian Muslim style which is almost the same as Kedah Laksa because it has sliced boiled egg on it. The original Penang Nyonya Asam Laksa does not have egg and lettuce but have alot of cliced raw onion in it. But then some people prefer this fusion way of it..

author: @christopheroliver5807
comment: I've just returned from a 2 month stay in Georgetown and found the food to have been just as amazing as always. Actually now seems to be another layer in between the street food and restaurant meals ie cafes serving fusion food plus local specialties for around the 12/15 ringgit mark. Little more expensive than street food but very good. Still think the great letdown with Georgetown is the seafood. I'm an Aussie so accustomed to high quality seafood. Never found a sweet prawn at all (5 attempts) this time around and fish always overcooked. As you said here I suppose it's mostly served in curries so it's inevitable.

author: @PisangBerangan508
comment:
As a Penang Chinese guy, three dishes that are on top of everything, they are Keow Teow Teng (Pork Soup Keow Teow), Wantan Mee (Pork mince Wantan with egg noodle), and the last one is Char Keow Teow.
They are the three Kings.
Then later there are Hokkien Mee (Prawn Soup Noodle), Asam Laksa and Curry Noodle, they are the three Dukes.
The the less famous but also delicious such as Keow Chap (Pork, Duck, Chicken based soup noodles), Jawa Mee (Tomato Soup Noodle), Char Tua Pan (Wok Fried Big Sizes Noodle served with gravy soup, Pork and Prawn based food). These are the three Counts. The remaining rest as above than average food from Chinese.
As for Malays and Indians food, they are colourful and coconut milk based, and very tasty.
So, after all delicious Penang food, maybe your body become so hot, you can drink green coconut water too. Ahha

author: @jbrkjferkjbekjb
comment:
I feel like the Nasi Lemak at the start is not just good cooking but more importantly genius packaging/marketing, by stacking the one side dish on top of the rice, wrapped beautifully in a banana leaf with sambal slathered on it. Something tells me it’s not that special in terms of flavour.

In most places that would be insufficient for Nasi Lemak, esp with just ikan bilis, egg, sambal, rice, but they made it look like a good quantity with genius packaging. In other places, the Nasi Lemak, sambal, and side dishes are served separately, so you can easily tell when the ingredients are few.

My fav Nasi Lemak place has rice, sambal, eggs, chicken, prawns, and otah. Some say it’s too much but not necessarily if the chef knows how to complement everything together. And no, the side dishes are all served separately (on the same plate but not stacked into a ‘ball’ like the video’s one) because the side dishes are tasty enough to eat on their own.

Penang Assam laksa is usually made by Chinese vendors and is non-halal, it has very dark broth, more sweet and with a much stronger fishy smell. Roti Jala is a crepe and a very northern Malaysian food from the Jawi people!

author: @paulttchen
comment:
Mark been watching your channel since your early days and always been a fan!!
I am a born and bred penangite who grew up in vancouver but moved back to PG and I absolutely love Penang ,it's food,culture,people !

I would like to recommend a few of my favs places and some are off the tourist traps where locals only know ,while some are not but still divine and a must try !!
Pls google them for the location.

1) Jalan Rambutan Laksa
(Old school style Chinese laksa with flavors of the past and perfectly balanced and pls add a lot of prawn paste for the added sweetness and umami)

2) Hot bowl white curry mee
Its in the Michelin guide but still one of the local favs and one of the best white curry mee
(Also order the hainan poached white chicken with their chicken rice and also order their lor bak(hainan 5spice meat rolls)

3)Loh Kei duck meat koay teow th'ng ( flat rice noodles in a divine duck broth with handmade fishballs. Please also order a plate of their tender duck slices with duck intestines and parts and also bean sprouts. A place that locals especially older gen patronised during breakfast and go there early cause they sell out fast.

4)Restoran Kapitan(chulia street)
One of the local favourite place to get bang for buck tandoori chicken with garlic cheese naan (many varieties of naan to choose from)

Chose a few places to recommend to u and I've got loads more of gems so can msg me if u need more Mark !

author: @helmiismail4081
comment:
Mark…!!! Please eat nasi kandar as simple as possible.. Nasi Kandar always eat with chicken tight..and dont take a lot of protein…tips please do this step..if you eat nasi kandar..
- Nasi Putih or Nasi Dalca
- Kuah Banjir
- Chicken tight ( ayam goreng tight or ayam bawang tight or ayam tanjung tight )
- Telur Masin ( salted egg ) the bigger the eggs york the better
- Telur bawang ( omelette )
- Kobis (cabbage)
- Bendi (Lady finger)
- Sambal nyok ( Coconut scradded sambal )
- Bawang Goreng ( fried onion ) must have

This is local choice..try this step just change protein..
Hope you you enjoy nasi kandar..

author: @MexicoAdventurer
comment:
Penang is my favorite place for Asian food. My hotel had the best fruit breakfast that got me going until lunch, then I just had to EAT SOLID FOOD! Seafood for both lunch and dinner... and for my late snack, was my preference.

There is a food court that I ate at, on my first day that I could not find again. It was pure white, and huge. White tent roof, white tables, white chairs, white restaurants. Very clean, too. There were about 30 different food vendors. I'm going to add that everyone eating there was dressed to the 9's, NOT POOR. It was the best food I'd had in Malaysia ANYWHERE.

I got to eat at the 8 story mall on the beach on Penang Island, and it was good, but not anywhere near the same as the street food at the big white outdoor food court food. I need to go visit again, just for the food.

author: @wpyeoh
comment:
I wanted to say that you got kinda scammed at the nasi kandar place, and so did everyone else, it seems. :P Hameediyah is one of the tastiest nasi kandar, but they always recommend all the expensive stuff. And most people would just take one expensive item if they really wanted it, but he recommended you all of them in one go. Not sure whether you thought it was worth getting "scammed", though; the dishes may really have that quality to them.

About the roti canai tsunami, it might have been a tourist "scam" as well; do try basic roti canai next time, personally I prefer dipping it into gravy right before eating, to preserve the crispness of the exterior, rather than having it totally soaked.

author: @bdb1416
comment:
On the chicken rice part, instead of people going to Hainan to work, it is the other way round where many of the Chinese such as those from Guangdong, Fujian, Hakka, Teochew, Hainan etc moved south (Nan-yang) and brought along the Chinese cuisines which they refine/localize according to local resources and/or environment/taste. Just like how the Chinese brought the Chinese food/culture & introduce/mix/assimilate with the local to have Nyonya food.

For an authentic/traditional cendol, the green cendol is supposed to be made of mung bean & natural pandan color (instead of artificial food coloring).

author: @selva2820
comment: I applaud your mandarin, nice!. However, this video has many misleading information to fellow travelers to Malaysia. To start with, the traditional roti canai of malaysia is far from what you tried.....chicken piece, eggs and cheese? what on earth combination is this? who introduced you to this?. author: @Cats-N-Music
comment:
Amy you nailed it with regards to chicken rice in Singapore and Malaysia. That concurs with my personal experience between the difference with modern chicken rice in both countries. Can't comment about China as i have no experience. 😂
Going back to Chicken rice in Singapore. I just wanna add something you might not be aware about.
In the 80s, you would be able to 2 types of Chicken rice in Singapore. One that was almost dry and one that was glazed in alot of soy based sauce and topped with long cuts of spring onion leaves very similar in look to what you had in this Penang episode. The former being Hainanese style and the latter being Cantonese style.
Of course, the OG would be Hainanese and one other way to identify Hainanese Chicken rice would be the dark soy dipping sauce, the vicosity of the dark soy sauce would be so thick that it is almost resisitant to flow and appears gooey when you drip it over your chicken.
Over the years into the 90s, you got to see less and less Cantonese style chicken rice and Hainanese chicken rice now had way more soy/ sesame based sauce, but still less wet than Cantonese chicken rice. It seemed that by the late 90s Cantonese chicken rice had slowly started to disappear in Singapore. Today what you find is no longer authentic Hainanese style chicken rice in Singapore but a fusion of the dish that incorporates traits of both styles. If you did like to know how Cantonese style chicken rice tasted like in the old days. Try Wee Nam Kee chicken rice along Thomson Road in Singapore. They brand themselves as Hainanese chicken rice but it is the closest thing i have ever found to the traditional Cantonese style chicken rice from the bygone days of Singapore. I would have suggested that you visited Yet Con at Purvis Street for traditional Hainanese Chicken rice as a base line to compare with modern day Hainanese chicken rice to see how much of a change it is but unfortunately they have ceased operations after almost 80yrs.
Anyway great content as usual and
Thanks for the wonderful food content in beautiful Penang! ❤

author: @steveliu99
comment:
The "mamak" you had with lamb, prawn..etc is not cheap. It's mostly a no-go for most Malaysians becoz they are absurdly expensive.
For the morning breakfast....hardly any local eats such hearty meals....most people do is eat 2 slices of "roti canai", serve with chicken curry and dhal on separate dishes (not poured everything into the same dishes). There are chinese coffee shops that has morning breakfast , which I think you had similar experience in Singapore on dipping toast breads into half-boiled eggs(splash with soy sauce/pepper) or kaya bread....or even coffee with butter mixed in.....
For dessert, Malaysia has a vast variety of them... from curry puff (almost like salmosa), to fried banana (banana fritters), yam balls,..etc to appam balik (pancakes), to Tau Sar Piah ( which is famous in Penang as well)...just need to walk the confectionery shops (mainly chinese), while some indian shops sell their cultural desserts....or market places where Malay some other pastries are sold.
Melaka/Malacca would also be a great place with multi varieties of such "kuih" pastries.....
There is way too much heritage and cultural food in Malaysia to go through in 2-5 videos....and that is just "West" Malaysia, without even touching the Borneo/East side...
Hence...I think once you scraped the top layer...your husband and parents could join in the adventure at some point...perhaps next year?....maybe plan them as well during the Malaysia festive seasons....i don't know if you want to see how Chinese New Year, Ramadan , Thaipusam celebrations is like....just avoid some of those "touristy" spots...

author: @TheOz91
comment:
Malaysia is weirdly one of those countries that people overlook—until recently—which is why you have so many people remark how Malaysia is "a country I've never been to before" despite having gone to Thailand, Singapore, and Indonesia. It's weird being a flyover country and I find it interesting that people find it interesting in the way that it is not like Thailand, Indonesia, or Singapore but something completely different.

As for the roti canai, the roti canai tsunami is a variation of roti banjir (flooded roti canai in gravy) and in other places it is also known as roti special. There are so many ways that roti canai is made into other dishes that people say roti canai by itself as "roti kosong" (literally "empty" roti) with the other dishes include roti telur (roti canai with egg folded into it), roti sardin (roti canai with mashed canned sardines put into it), and roti bom ("bomb" roti, basically roti canai with sugar and margerine put into it and folded together slightly different and cooked a bit crispier). By the way, RM13 is actually quite a lot for a majority of Malaysians for just breakfast; the more regular roti and teh tarik would come up to between RM5 and RM7 in a shop like that.

So, for a bit of history of teh tarik, we can look back to British colonisation. It is not just strong tea but a tea that has a high tanine content, so it has to be specific kind of tea. During the British colonial era, Cameron Highlands is where tea started to be cultivated and it is high quality tea. The leaves were sold at a pretty penny, expensive enough that the locals were priced out. However, the tea dust that were left behind were dirt cheap and this contains the tanine which gives the tea an extra strong quality flavor to it. And you can always order it less sweet in case you keep finding it too sweet.

Another thing about Malaysia is that not all the ethnic Chinese people can speak Mandarin. Georgetown (well, Penang in general) as well as KL are a lot more focused on speaking Mandarin. However, there are many other groups of Chinese speakers including Hokkien and Cantonese, so in some places you might see ethnic Chinese people speaking English or Malay to each other.

Yes, the dark sugar that is used on cendol is palm sugar. There are generally two types of cendol, one is a drink you can enjoy in a cup and the other is one shaved ice. So yes, drinking cendol is perfectly 100% valid.

Lastly, of course, you can't be in Penang and not have nasi kandar. Penang is indeed the home of nasi kandar and enjoyed by pretty much everyone. "Kuah campur" (mixed gravy) is basically mandatory when eating nasi kandar, regardless of what you have on it. They will always, always, ALWAYS try to upsell you so be firm if you don't want something since it can get really pricey really fast. I do recommend having a vegetable dish next time because the crunch of the veggies is heavenly with the gravy soaked rice.

author: @nicekit
comment:
hi Amy
Malaysian is nationality , Indian, Chinese and Malay are the races
so Mamak is a Indian + Malay race
Peranakan is the Chinese + Malay race

Nyonya is the Matriarch of a Peranakan family
Baba is the Patriarch of a Peranakan family
but ppl do use Nyonya food but technically ... it is Peranakan food.


the cendol that u have is a street style cendol.. but if u go to a sit down cendol
it will most probably look like the 'replica' cuz u cant eat that standing up ahahhaa
the 'brown' sugar isnt brown sugar.. but it is Penang's Palm Sugar... also usually known as Gula Melaka malaca sugar
i never seen any other country that have dark brown palm sugar other than malaysia and indonesia...
thailand's palm sugar is lighter in color and not as intense in flavour..
in malaysia, our parents WILL never let us have cendol for breakfast hahahahaha, it is only for dessert and during hot humid days treats.

the hot putu full name is putu piring.. i dun remember putu means what
but piring means plate where the small little plate where there's a hole where the steam will cook the rice flour
it is meant to be eaten immediately while it is warm.. because when it is packed, it will have water condensation and all mush together..
inside also have.... u guessed it.. gula melaka...

author: @paulttchen
comment:
Welcome to my hometown !!
Land of heritage and multinational food capital of malaysia !!

1) Hameediyah may be old but they well known to rip people off and especially tourist like what you experienced . There are many many others that taste better , much more affordable and just as authentic.

2) For roti canai , should always start with a plain one that comes with a side of curry so one can taste the crispiness and dip as much curry as one likes to unlike the tsunami where they drench it .

3) There are so many more foods for you to try such as curry mee( red or white curry mee), Hokkien prawn noodle , koay teow soup thng ( kuey teow aka flat rice noodle in pork or duck based broth), bah kut teh( pork broth tea soup with parts and you tiao), and many many more penang specialties.

There also malay specialties and also other favourite indian cuisines thats indigenous to malaysia.

Next time when you order drinks, try ordering the freshly squeezed juices , nutmeg juice, ambra juice, and hor ka sai( milo + coffee ).
There are a plethora of very local drinks to explore .

Anyways happy to see you visiting malaysia !!

author: @yeewengchiang3295
comment: Wow! Blondie finally in Malaysia! So exciting!! Welcome!! 欢迎 😃😃😃👏👏👏Actually, there is no such thing as PURE ___ (whatever race) food here. You would find a mixture of hybrid fusion of different influences in all these food. You have Malay and India influences in the local Chinese food; Chinese influences in both Malay or Indian food etc... it's just ONE huge melting pot of food cultures heaven here. BTW, I think the Roti Tsunami is a bit of an overkill. I personally prefer the traditional, conventional way - just the bread with the sauce... You would find that Malaysians like this kind of crazy stuff- to "flood" their food with different sauces, and to overflow their plate with all sort of combo... it's kind of messy and not necessarily the best way to enjoying the food. But then again, people are free to eat in whatever way they enjoy the best. And you MUST have those huge Blood clams (Si Ham) in the Char Kuey Teow!!! Anyway, Have a great trip and hope you enjoy the food here (and come again!) 😉🙂🙂

author: @swethaarabandi
comment:
The chai in the first place comes from South India. The way they pour it is a way to mix the tea and all of the flavors together. You can find it in a lot of street stalls in Hyderabad and Chennai and the surrounding areas.

A lot of Indian food use a spice mixture in hot oil. That’s what makes a lot of dishes flavorful and unique. In Telugu, it’s called thalimpu. In Hindi, it is known as tadka.

Those little steamed rice cake cakes are similar to something called idli and are a popular breakfast item in South India, especially in the home. We usually eat it with coconut chutney, peanut chutney, sambar, and/or karam podi (spice blend powder).

You might be able to find some Indo Chinese food in Malaysia. It’s so delicious.

author: @simplyme3306
comment:
The cendol is not just green colouring. The good ones will use those made with just pandan juice. I think there are better cendols out there than the popular one you tried. The Hainanese chicken rice is the result of the Hainanese from China arriving in Singapore/Malaysia and they created their own version in this region. I would have actually wanted you to try the roti as is in original form first dip in curry or sugar. The "Tsunami" is a modern version and I personally won't like it as the roti will be soggy from all the sauces it soaked in.
For nonya/peranakan food, you can try and compare them from Penang, Melaka and Singapore - where the nonya people resides. Also because these are where most of the Chinese population are. Ipoh is another place where most Chinese resides. You have to try their Ipoh kway tiao and bean sprouts. It's due to their spring water, the taste and texture are just the best.
There's no toufu in the char kway teow and it's usually chives not spring onions.

Finally in Malaysia tasting the real stuff.
6 meals a day : breakfast, elevensies, lunch, tea time, dinner, then supper at a mamak. Sounds about right. Just so you know diabetes is an endemic in Malaysia, I’d watch the sugar intake.

Waaaaa welcome to malaysia! Hope you enjoy your stay!

Happy to see you enjoyed the roti canai and nasi kandar, but they do make you buy the expensive ones 😭

Roti canai is better when it’s plain because you can taste both the crunchy and chewy texture without them getting soggy! Other varieties is great too if you wanna feel fuller, get the roti telur/roti tampal (egg roti) there also more varieties depending on your liking!

As for the cendol, you do got it right the first time! Some stall prefer thicker coconut milk with more ice ratio and enjoy them like shaved ice, and some make it more watery and enjoy them as beverages. I personally prefer to add creamy corn and peanuts because they add a lot to the flavour!

We’re proud of our nasi lemak and bak kut teh here too! Curious if you tried that while visiting😆

author: @RichardsonLau
comment:
You’ve got the origins of Chicken Rice a bit mixed up. It was the Hainanese migrants who brought the dish from Hainan to Singapore, Malaysia, and other places. They arrived later than other Chinese groups, such as those from Quanzhou, Chaoshan, and other coastal areas. By the time the Hainanese came, the coolie trade was already saturated, and opportunities in commerce—like the tea and Chinese goods trade—were limited. As a result, many of them found work as cooks for the British colonial households. This eventually led them to venture into the food and beverage industry.

Their entry into this space gave rise to several uniquely Hainanese culinary creations that are distinct to Singapore and Malaysia. Among them was their localized version of Wenchang chicken, adapted using local poultry and ingredients. Naturally, the dish evolved differently in various regions due to the availability of ingredients and local preferences.

author: @IsaacRothberg
comment: Have there been past videos too where it seemed like it was filmed same day but wasn’t, or seemed like it was happening kind of continuously but there was a lot of time and walking in between? I absolutely love your channel, I’ve been a loyal viewer for years. But one thing that has always upset me a little about many food/travel content creators is the quantity of food they seem to be consuming. I mean, I understand that there are some people who can put a lot of food away in a given meal or maybe a given day! Lol. But there’s gotta be a limit. And as you said, you felt the need to wear the same outfit for a three day visit to Penang to sort of make it look like it was all done in one day. I guess I just don’t think that on balance, there is an appeal in the food binge/mukbang thing when it comes to smart, knowledgeable, travel-oriented videos like yours. I never felt the kind of bewilderment of like, how can this person possible eat this much, when watching all of Anthony Bourdain’s many shows. But it is a common theme in YouTube and it seems like creators are being intentional in making content that emphasizes quantity of food consumption. Anyway, hadn’t commented before, but just wanted to share that because it got raised explicitly in this video. And to be clear I certainly don’t get upset about that watching all over you videos 😂 but some of them I am like what the absolute heck how can she do this! Whatever the case, thank you for all the wonderful content about China you have given us. Honestly you are one of the best windows into Chinese life and culture for English speaking audiences that is available anywhere, period. And for whatever the many drawbacks there are to the Chinese political model, I always recommend your channel to friends as a reference to see how much amazing development and progress has occurred in China, how much the richness of the culture there can thrive and grow and innovate these days because of that development and progress. You are living the dream. Reminds me that maybe I’ve gotta get back to Mexico at some point. Or Vietnam. Or China! Life is short!

author: @HumbleBee25
comment:
So glad you enjoyed the food binge but personally I'd recommend that the roti canai with just fish, dhall & chicken curry gravy served separately in shallow saucers; not tsunami styled! For cendol, I usually ask for less gula Melaka (less overpowering sweetness) & not too much shaved ice so that I can get coconut milk's creaminess. The beans are actually a late 20th century addition. The other traditional add-ons was pieces of steamed glutinous rice (pulut) or attap chee/seed (a kind of nipah palm gelatinous fruit). In my opinion, Nasi Kandar is best enjoyed with not more than 3 meat dish, one veggie & one curry gravy that should not obscure/distract the flavours of the meats/veggie. I also try not to have flavoured drinks with my meals & wd go for cold/lukewarm water or mild hot Chinese tea. Apart from the clash of the food's flavour, all milk-based, carbonated drinks or fruit juices may cause stomach upsets or bloating. I stick to these principles especially when I'm trying new cuisines or unfamiliar food outlets.
Have not seen the rest of your Msian food adventure clips yet but do try Malay food from other states like Kelantan, Negri Sembilan, etc even if you're mainly on the West coast states. Bon appetit 😋

author: @hlt2831
comment:
Lovely episode! Haha have been looking forward to your Malaysia series. Fun recollection: When I was a young kid here in Malaysia, we called the basil seeds in the ais tingkap "frog eggs". Gave us a thrill, almost like our own little fear factor experience.

My thoughts on the chicken rice, I am a Malaysian of Hainanese descent – my grandma who came from Hainan island instilled in us a love for the tougher chicken because they are usually free range and have lots more flavour. In Malaysia, some hainanese chicken rice stalls/restaurants offer both regular chicken and the more expensive free range chicken of certain breeds like bearded chicken or village chicken.

As for the Indian food here, we have both Indian food (they are usually Hindus and some are Christians) and also Mamak food (they are Indian Muslims, not Indian + Malays).

Apart from the Chinese Peranakan (intermarriage of Chinese settlers with local people in the Malay Peninsula between the 15th and 17th centuries), there are also the Jawi Peranakan (intermarriage of Indian muslims settlers and local Malays in 19th century), Chetty or Indian Peranakan (intermarriage of South Indian Hindu settlers and local Malays) and Kristang or Eurasian Peranakan (intermarriage of Portuguese and locals during the Portuguese colonial period). Each have their own culture and cuisine. (Note: I am not completely sure of the timeline)

Thanks for taking us along for the ride. Looking forward to next week's video.

author: @carsongaming3043
comment:
as a Malaysian, I just gotta say all these dishes taste are kinda based on the seller itself as different states have different tastes... like for example some parts in Kuala lumpur, the nasi lemak sambal is a bit more sweet than spicy... and Assam laksa in Johor is spicy while authentic Assam laksa from Penang is a bit more sweet and savory and not that spicy as they add more of a thing called "har ko" (prawn paste)... same with char koey teow Penang is more famous and nicer with the wok hey ( charcoal fried kuey teow )

since we have 3 main ethnicities.. Chinese, malay and indian
malay and indian like more spicy spicy food like cili padi and sambal while Chinese like spicy but not that spicy with a mix of either sour/sweet or sometimes even salty

author: @trevvv1417
comment:
The World's Spiciest Pan Mee is not just a dish—it's a life decision.
I tried it once, and I swear on my burning soul—it deserves the title.

At first bite? Harmless. “Hey, this isn’t too bad,” you’ll think.
But then it creeps in.
Like a demon doing jumping jacks in your mouth.
And just when you think it’s over, it goes underground—straight to your stomach.

That’s where the real horror begins.
Minutes after I finished, my insides were screaming. I couldn’t even stand. I was just folded on my bed like a cooked noodle, rethinking my life choices.

And then... the Final Boss: the toilet showdown.
Let’s just say—you don’t poop fire, the fire poops you. 🔥💀

The taste? Glorious. But I now understand the phrase “delicious suffering.”
Eat at your own risk… and maybe write a will first. 😂🔥

author: @momon8738
comment:
Asam pedas is an interesting dish indeed. It is a classic malay dish, also exist in Indonesia where the descendant of malay tribe live like Riau, Riau Island, Sumatera Barat, etc. Although it looks like simple dish with strong point of a little sourness and spicy from chilli. But all version taste different. Personally, I prefer the version of malaka/malaysia/riau island since my father grew in riau island. It used sundried red chili and two kinds of asam fruit called asam gelugur and if possible asam jawa or simply asam kandis. It used lots more chili than the Malaysia and sumatera barat variant. Red chili is not a spicy chili compared to like birds eye chili, but since it used lots of it, it is spicy and numby from extra pepper. The asam fruit adds hint of sourness but the most important is the smell and taste is like forcing us to eat more

That leafs in the soup on the 2nd restaurant looks like "daun katuk" or google says "In English, daun katuk is known as sweet leaf or star gooseberry."; or maybe daun kelor (moringa leaves). But based on your description, it sounds like daun katuk. You're not wrong, it has unique somekind of sweet taste and spinach like aroma and texture.

author: @McBandycars
comment:
I love this video but first I REALLY must ask if you've ever had Bell's Palsy. I never had any nerve damage until experienced some great Foreign food in Mexico and then swimming through a crystal clear water cave and lazy stream. I assumed it was always from all the stress, which is what leads to Bell's Palsy a lot of the time.

With all that said, it really doesn't matter unless you want to learn facial muscle exercises for help. Mine caused damage to 1 eye and my vision is permanently messed up in that eye.

Before I end rant, I love spice and I have used it in combination with other things to help revive nerves! Capsaicin works in conjunction with so many things in the body, but I'll end it here before I type forever.

Keep on enjoying life Mark and thank you for all your videos! I enjoy your reactions, most people think I am insane for some of the stuff I've tried, most people from the Midwest aren't as inclined to eat anything crazy unless you think crazy is hunting & cleaning and/or finding your own freshly deceased /not rotten and cleaning it for dinner, or even smoking it for rations. That is pretty normal here. =P

Stay safe all, not all weird food is safe! Mark knows this, he's had food poisoning just like all of us easily tempted exotic food lovers have been! Seafood poisoning still to this day is something I fear. =P
